text.NRSV: Paul, a servant of Jesus Christ, called to be an apostle, set apart for the gospel of God,
text.Rotherham: Paul, a servant of Jesus Christ, a called apostle, separated unto the glad-message of God—

text.NRSV: which he promised beforehand through his prophets in the holy scriptures,
text.Rotherham: Which he promised beforehand, through his prophets, in holy scriptures—

text.NRSV: the gospel concerning his Son, who was descended from David according to the flesh
text.Rotherham: Concerning his Son,—who came to be of the seed of David, according to flesh,

text.NRSV: and was declared to be Son of God with power according to the spirit of holiness by resurrection from the dead, Jesus Christ our Lord,
text.Rotherham: Who was distinguished as the Son of God—by power, according to a Holy Spirit, through means of a resurrection of the dead,—Jesus Christ our Lord;

text.NRSV: through whom we have received grace and apostleship to bring about the obedience of faith among all the Gentiles for the sake of his name,
text.Rotherham: Through whom we have received favour and apostleship, for obedience of faith among all the nations, in behalf of his name,

text.NRSV: including yourselves who are called to belong to Jesus Christ,
text.Rotherham: Among whom are, ye also, called of Jesus Christ:

text.NRSV: To all God’s beloved in Rome, who are called to be saints: Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
text.Rotherham: Unto all that are in Rome, beloved of God, called saints, favour unto you, and peace, from God our Father and Lord Jesus Christ.

text.NRSV: First, I thank my God through Jesus Christ for all of you, because your faith is proclaimed throughout the world.
text.Rotherham: First, indeed, I give thanks unto my God, through Jesus Christ, concerning you all, because your faith is being announced throughout the whole world.

text.NRSV: For God, whom I serve with my spirit by announcing the gospel of his Son, is my witness that without ceasing I remember you always in my prayers,
text.Rotherham: For God is, my witness,—unto whom I am rendering divine service in my spirit, in the glad message of his Son,—how incessantly, I am making mention of you

text.NRSV: asking that by God’s will I may somehow at last succeed in coming to you.
text.Rotherham: At all times in my prayers,—making supplication—if, by some means, even now, at any time, I may have a way opened, in the will of God, to come unto you;

text.NRSV: For I am longing to see you so that I may share with you some spiritual gift to strengthen you—
text.Rotherham: For I am longing to see you, that I may impart some spiritual gift unto you, to the end ye may be established,—

text.NRSV: or rather so that we may be mutually encouraged by each other’s faith, both yours and mine.
text.Rotherham: That is to say—there may be a mutual encouragement among you, each by the other’s faith, both yours and mine.

text.NRSV: I want you to know, brothers and sisters, that I have often intended to come to you (but thus far have been prevented), in order that I may reap some harvest among you as I have among the rest of the Gentiles.
text.Rotherham: I do not wish however that ye should be ignorant, brethren, that, many times, have I purposed to come unto you, but have been hindered, until the present,—in order that, some fruit, I might have among you also, even as among the other nations,

text.NRSV: I am a debtor both to Greeks and to barbarians, both to the wise and to the foolish;
text.Rotherham: Both to Greeks and to Barbarians, both to wise and to unwise, a debtor, I am:

text.NRSV: hence my eagerness to proclaim the gospel to you also who are in Rome.
text.Rotherham: Thus, the eagerness on my part—unto you also who are in Rome, to announce the joyful message.

text.NRSV: For I am not ashamed of the gospel; it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who has faith, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.
text.Rotherham: For I am not ashamed of the joyful message; for it is God’s power unto salvation, to every one that believeth, both to Jew [first] and to Greek;

text.NRSV: For in it the righteousness of God is revealed through faith for faith; as it is written, “The one who is righteous will live by faith.”
text.Rotherham: For, a righteousness of God, is therein revealed,—by faith unto faith: even as it is written—But, he that is righteous, by faith, shall live.

text.NRSV: For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and wickedness of those who by their wickedness suppress the truth.
text.Rotherham: For there is being revealed an anger of God from heaven—against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men who, the truth, in unrighteousness, do hold down;—

text.NRSV: For what can be known about God is plain to them, because God has shown it to them.
text.Rotherham: Inasmuch as, what may be known of God, is manifest among them, for, God, unto them, hath made it manifest,—

text.NRSV: Ever since the creation of the world his eternal power and divine nature, invisible though they are, have been understood and seen through the things he has made. So they are without excuse;
text.Rotherham: For, the unseen things of him, from a world’s creation, by the things made, being perceived, are clearly seen, even his eternal power and divinity,—to the end they should be without excuse;

text.NRSV: for though they knew God, they did not honor him as God or give thanks to him, but they became futile in their thinking, and their senseless minds were darkened.
text.Rotherham: Inasmuch as, having come to know God, not, as God, did they glorify him, or give him thanks, but were made fruitless in their reasonings, and darkened was their undiscerning heart,

text.NRSV: Claiming to be wise, they became fools;
text.Rotherham: Professing to be wise, they were made foolish,

text.NRSV: and they exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images resembling a mortal human being or birds or four-footed animals or reptiles.
text.Rotherham: And, exchanged the glory of the incorruptible God, for the likeness of an image of a corruptible man, and of birds and fourfooted beasts and reptiles:

text.NRSV: Therefore God gave them up in the lusts of their hearts to impurity, to the degrading of their bodies among themselves,
text.Rotherham: Wherefore God gave them up in the covetings of their hearts unto impurity, so as to be dishonouring their bodies among them,—

text.NRSV: because they exchanged the truth about God for a lie and worshiped and served the creature rather than the Creator, who is blessed forever! Amen.
text.Rotherham: Who, indeed, exchanged away the truth of God for the falsehood, and rendered worship and service unto the creature rather than unto the Creator,—who is blessed unto the ages. Amen!

text.NRSV: For this reason God gave them up to degrading passions. Their women exchanged natural intercourse for unnatural,
text.Rotherham: For this cause, God gave them up unto dishonourable passions; for, even their females, exchanged away the natural use into that which is against nature,—

text.NRSV: and in the same way also the men, giving up natural intercourse with women, were consumed with passion for one another. Men committed shameless acts with men and received in their own persons the due penalty for their error.
text.Rotherham: In like manner also, even the males, leaving the natural use of the female, flamed out in their eager desire one for another, males with males, the indecency, effecting,—and, the necessary recompence of their error, within themselves, duly receiving;—

text.NRSV: And since they did not see fit to acknowledge God, God gave them up to a debased mind and to things that should not be done.
text.Rotherham: And, even as they did not approve to be holding, God, in acknowledgment, God gave them up unto a disapproved mind to be doing the things that are not becoming,

text.NRSV: They were filled with every kind of wickedness, evil, covetousness, malice. Full of envy, murder, strife, deceit, craftiness, they are gossips,
text.Rotherham: Filled with all unrighteousness, wickedness, greed, baseness, full of envy, murder, strife, deceit, evil disposition,

text.NRSV: slanderers, God-haters, insolent, haughty, boastful, inventors of evil, rebellious toward parents,
text.Rotherham: Whisperers, detractors, haters of God, insolent, arrogant, vain boasters, inventors of vices, unto parents unyielding,

text.NRSV: foolish, faithless, heartless, ruthless.
text.Rotherham: Without discernment, regardless of covenants, without natural affection, unmerciful:—

text.NRSV: They know God’s decree, that those who practice such things deserve to die—yet they not only do them but even applaud others who practice them.
text.Rotherham: Who, indeed, having acknowledged the righteous sentence of God,—that, they who such things as these do practise, are worthy of death, not only, the same things, are doing, but are even delighting together with them who are practising [them].

text.NRSV: Therefore you have no excuse, whoever you are, when you judge others; for in passing judgment on another you condemn yourself, because you, the judge, are doing the very same things.
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, inexcusable, thou art, O man, whoever judgest; for, wherein thou judgest some one else, thyself, thou dost condemn,—for, the very things, thou dost practise, who art judging:

text.NRSV: You say, “We know that God’s judgment on those who do such things is in accordance with truth.”
text.Rotherham: We know, however, that, the sentence of God, is according to truth against them who, such things as these, do practise.

text.NRSV: Do you imagine, whoever you are, that when you judge those who do such things and yet do them yourself, you will escape the judgment of God?
text.Rotherham: And reckonest thou this, O man—who dost judge them who such things do practise, and yet art doing the same, that, thou, shalt escape the sentence of God?

text.NRSV: Or do you despise the riches of his kindness and forbearance and patience? Do you not realize that God’s kindness is meant to lead you to repentance?
text.Rotherham: Or, the riches of his kindness, and forbearance, and long-suffering, dost thou despise,—not knowing that, the kindness of God, unto repentance, is leading thee?

text.NRSV: But by your hard and impenitent heart you are storing up wrath for yourself on the day of wrath, when God’s righteous judgment will be revealed.
text.Rotherham: But, according to thy hardness, and [thine] impenitent heart, art treasuring up for thyself anger, in a day of anger and revelation of the righteous judgment of God,—

text.NRSV: For he will repay according to each one’s deeds:
text.Rotherham: Who will render unto each one according to his works:—

text.NRSV: to those who by patiently doing good seek for glory and honor and immortality, he will give eternal life;
text.Rotherham: Unto them, on the one hand, who, by way of endurance in good work, are seeking, glory, honour and incorruption, life age-abiding,

text.NRSV: while for those who are self-seeking and who obey not the truth but wickedness, there will be wrath and fury.
text.Rotherham: Unto them, on the other hand, who are of contention, and are not yielding unto the truth, but are yielding unto unrighteousness, anger and wrath,

text.NRSV: There will be anguish and distress for everyone who does evil, the Jew first and also the Greek,
text.Rotherham: tribulation and anguish—against every soul of man who worketh out what is base, both of Jew first and of Greek,—

text.NRSV: but glory and honor and peace for everyone who does good, the Jew first and also the Greek.
text.Rotherham: But glory and honour and peace—unto every one who worketh what is good, both unto Jew first and unto Greek:

text.NRSV: For God shows no partiality.
text.Rotherham: For there is no respect of persons with God;—

text.NRSV: All who have sinned apart from the law will also perish apart from the law, and all who have sinned under the law will be judged by the law.
text.Rotherham: For, as many as without law sinned, without law, also shall perish, and, as many as within law sinned, through law, shall be judged;

text.NRSV: For it is not the hearers of the law who are righteous in God’s sight, but the doers of the law who will be justified.
text.Rotherham: For, not the hearers of law, are righteous with God, but, the doers of law, shall be declared righteous;

text.NRSV: When Gentiles, who do not possess the law, do instinctively what the law requires, these, though not having the law, are a law to themselves.
text.Rotherham: For, whensoever the nations which have not law, by nature, the things of the law, may be doing, the same, not having law, unto themselves, are a law,—

text.NRSV: They show that what the law requires is written on their hearts, to which their own conscience also bears witness; and their conflicting thoughts will accuse or perhaps excuse them
text.Rotherham: Who, indeed, shew the work of the law written in their hearts, their conscience therewith bearing witness, and, between one another, their reasonings accusing—or, even excusing, them:—

text.NRSV: on the day when, according to my gospel, God, through Jesus Christ, will judge the secret thoughts of all.
text.Rotherham: In the day on which God judgeth the secrets of men according to my glad-message through Christ Jesus.

text.NRSV: But if you call yourself a Jew and rely on the law and boast of your relation to God
text.Rotherham: If, however, thou, art taking the name of Jew, and resting thyself upon law, and boasting in God,

text.NRSV: and know his will and determine what is best because you are instructed in the law,
text.Rotherham: And art taking note of his will, and testing the things that differ—when receiving oral instruction out of the law,

text.NRSV: and if you are sure that you are a guide to the blind, a light to those who are in darkness,
text.Rotherham: Art persuaded, moreover, that, thou thyself, art—a guide of the blind, a light of them that are in darkness,

text.NRSV: a corrector of the foolish, a teacher of children, having in the law the embodiment of knowledge and truth,
text.Rotherham: A trainer of the simple, a teacher of babes, having the forming of knowledge and truth in the law,—

text.NRSV: you, then, that teach others, will you not teach yourself? While you preach against stealing, do you steal?
text.Rotherham: Thou, therefore, that art teaching someone else, thyself, art thou not teaching? Thou that proclaimest—Do not steal! Art thou, stealing?

text.NRSV: You that forbid adultery, do you commit adultery? You that abhor idols, do you rob temples?
text.Rotherham: That sayest—Do not commit adultery! Art thou, committing adultery? That abhorrest sacrilege, Art thou, robbing temples?

text.NRSV: You that boast in the law, do you dishonor God by breaking the law?
text.Rotherham: That in law dost boast, Through the transgression of the law, art thou dishonouring God?

text.NRSV: For, as it is written, “The name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you.”
text.Rotherham: For the name of God, because of you, is defamed among the nations,—even as it is written.

text.NRSV: Circumcision indeed is of value if you obey the law; but if you break the law, your circumcision has become uncircumcision.
text.Rotherham: For, circumcision, indeed, profiteth—if, law, thou be practising; but, if thou be a transgressor of law, thy circumcision, hath become, uncircumcision

text.NRSV: So, if those who are uncircumcised keep the righteous requirements of the law, will not their uncircumcision be regarded as circumcision?
text.Rotherham: If then, the uncircumcision, be guarding the righteous requirement of the law, shall not, his uncircumcision, as circumcision be reckoned?—

text.NRSV: Then those who are physically uncircumcised but keep the law will condemn you that have the written code and circumcision but break the law.
text.Rotherham: And the uncircumcision by nature, completing the law, shall judge, thee, who, notwithstanding letter and circumcision, art a transgressor of law!

text.NRSV: For a person is not a Jew who is one outwardly, nor is true circumcision something external and physical.
text.Rotherham: For, not he who is one in appearance, is a Jew, nor is, that which is such in appearance in flesh, circumcision;

text.NRSV: Rather, a person is a Jew who is one inwardly, and real circumcision is a matter of the heart—it is spiritual and not literal. Such a person receives praise not from others but from God.
text.Rotherham: But, he who is one in secret, is a Jew,—and, [that is], circumcision, which is of the heart, in spirit, not in letter,—whose, praise, is not of men, but of God.

text.NRSV: Then what advantage has the Jew? Or what is the value of circumcision?
text.Rotherham: What, then, is the preeminence of the Jew? Or what, the profit of his circumcision?

text.NRSV: Much in every way. To begin with, the Jews were entrusted with the oracles of God.
text.Rotherham: Much, every way:—First, indeed, that they were entrusted with the oracles of God.

text.NRSV: What if some were unfaithful? Will their unfaithfulness nullify the faithfulness of God?
text.Rotherham: For what? If some distrusted, shall their distrust make, the trust of God, void?

text.NRSV: By no means! Although everyone is a liar, let God be proved true, as it is written, “So that you may be justified in your words, and prevail in your judging.”
text.Rotherham: Far be it! But let, God, prove to be, true, albeit, every man, be false! Even as it is written—That thou mightest be declared righteous in thy words, and overcome when thou art in judgment.

text.NRSV: But if our injustice serves to confirm the justice of God, what should we say? That God is unjust to inflict wrath on us? (I am speaking in a human way.)
text.Rotherham: But, if, our unrighteousness, commendeth, God’s righteousness, what shall we say? Surely, not unrighteous, is God who visiteth with his anger? After the manner of men, I am speaking,—

text.NRSV: By no means! For then how could God judge the world?
text.Rotherham: Far be it! Else how shall God judge the world?

text.NRSV: But if through my falsehood God’s truthfulness abounds to his glory, why am I still being condemned as a sinner?
text.Rotherham: But, if, the truth of God, by my falsehood, hath the more abounded unto his glory, why, any longer, am, even I, as a sinner, to be judged?

text.NRSV: And why not say (as some people slander us by saying that we say), “Let us do evil so that good may come”? Their condemnation is deserved!
text.Rotherham: And why not, according as we are injuriously charged, [and] according as some affirm that we say, Let us do the bad things, that the good ones may come? whose sentence is, just.

text.NRSV: What then? Are we any better off? No, not at all; for we have already charged that all, both Jews and Greeks, are under the power of sin,
text.Rotherham: What then? do we screen ourselves? Not at all! For we have before accused both Jews and Greeks of being, all under sin;

text.NRSV: as it is written: “There is no one who is righteous, not even one;
text.Rotherham: Even as it is written—there is none righteous, not even one,

text.NRSV: there is no one who has understanding, there is no one who seeks God.
text.Rotherham: There is none that discerneth, There is none that seeketh out God:

text.NRSV: All have turned aside, together they have become worthless; there is no one who shows kindness, there is not even one.”
text.Rotherham: All, have turned aside, together, have become useless, There is none that doeth kindness, not so much as one:

text.NRSV: “Their throats are opened graves; they use their tongues to deceive.” “The venom of vipers is under their lips.”
text.Rotherham: A sepulchre opened, is their throat, with their tongues, have they used deceit, the poison of asps, is under their lips,

text.NRSV: “Their mouths are full of cursing and bitterness.”
text.Rotherham: Whose mouth, of cursing and bitterness, is full,

text.NRSV: “Their feet are swift to shed blood;
text.Rotherham: Swift, are their feet to shed blood;

text.NRSV: ruin and misery are in their paths,
text.Rotherham: Destruction and misery, are in their ways,

text.NRSV: and the way of peace they have not known.”
text.Rotherham: And, the way of peace, have they not known:

text.NRSV: “There is no fear of God before their eyes.”
text.Rotherham: There is no fear of God before their eyes.

text.NRSV: Now we know that whatever the law says, it speaks to those who are under the law, so that every mouth may be silenced, and the whole world may be held accountable to God.
text.Rotherham: Now we know that, whatsoever things the law saith, to them who are within the law, it speaketh, in order that, every mouth, may be stopped and all the world come, under penal sentence, unto God.

text.NRSV: For “no human being will be justified in his sight” by deeds prescribed by the law, for through the law comes the knowledge of sin.
text.Rotherham: Inasmuch as, by works of law, shall no flesh be declared righteous before him,—through law, in fact, is discovery of sin.

text.NRSV: But now, apart from law, the righteousness of God has been disclosed, and is attested by the law and the prophets,
text.Rotherham: But now, apart from law, a righteousness of God hath been manifested, borne witness to by the law and the prophets,—

text.NRSV: the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe. For there is no distinction,
text.Rotherham: A righteousness of God, through faith in [Jesus] Christ, unto all that have faith; for there is no distinction,—

text.NRSV: since all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God;
text.Rotherham: For, all, have sinned and fall short of the glory of God;

text.NRSV: they are now justified by his grace as a gift, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus,
text.Rotherham: Being declared righteous freely by his favour through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus:—

text.NRSV: whom God put forward as a sacrifice of atonement by his blood, effective through faith. He did this to show his righteousness, because in his divine forbearance he had passed over the sins previously committed;
text.Rotherham: Whom God hath set forth as a propitiatory covering, through faith in his blood, for a showing forth of his righteousness, by reason of the passing-by of the previously committed sins,

text.NRSV: it was to prove at the present time that he himself is righteous and that he justifies the one who has faith in Jesus.
text.Rotherham: In the forbearance of God,—with a view to a showing forth of his righteousness in the present season, that he might be righteous even when declaring righteous him that hath faith in Jesus.

text.NRSV: Then what becomes of boasting? It is excluded. By what law? By that of works? No, but by the law of faith.
text.Rotherham: Where, then, the boasting! It is excluded. Through what kind of law? Of works? Nay! but through a law of faith:

text.NRSV: For we hold that a person is justified by faith apart from works prescribed by the law.
text.Rotherham: For we reckon that a man is to be declared righteous by faith, apart from works of law.

text.NRSV: Or is God the God of Jews only? Is he not the God of Gentiles also? Yes, of Gentiles also,
text.Rotherham: Or, [is God] the God, of Jews only, and not of the nations also? Yea! of the nations also:—

text.NRSV: since God is one; and he will justify the circumcised on the ground of faith and the uncircumcised through that same faith.
text.Rotherham: If, at all events, God is one, who will declare righteous—the circumcision by faith, and the uncircumcision through their faith.

text.NRSV: Do we then overthrow the law by this faith? By no means! On the contrary, we uphold the law.
text.Rotherham: Do we then make, law, void through means of our faith? Far be it! On the contrary, law, we do establish!

text.NRSV: What then are we to say was gained by Abraham, our ancestor according to the flesh?
text.Rotherham: What then shall we say—as touching Abraham our forefather?

text.NRSV: For if Abraham was justified by works, he has something to boast about, but not before God.
text.Rotherham: For, if Abraham by works was declared righteous, he hath whereof to boast;—nevertheless, not towards God,—

text.NRSV: For what does the scripture say? “Abraham believed God, and it was reckoned to him as righteousness.”
text.Rotherham: For what doth the Scripture say? And Abraham believed in God, and it was reckoned unto him as righteousness.

text.NRSV: Now to one who works, wages are not reckoned as a gift but as something due.
text.Rotherham: Now, unto him that worketh, the reward is not reckoned by way of favour but by way of obligation,

text.NRSV: But to one who without works trusts him who justifies the ungodly, such faith is reckoned as righteousness.
text.Rotherham: Whereas, unto him that worketh not but believeth on him that declareth righteous the ungodly, his faith is reckoned as righteousness.

text.NRSV: So also David speaks of the blessedness of those to whom God reckons righteousness apart from works:
text.Rotherham: Just as David also affirmeth the happiness of the man unto whom God reckoneth righteousness apart from works:—

text.NRSV: “Blessed are those whose iniquities are forgiven, and whose sins are covered;
text.Rotherham: Happy, they whose lawlessnesses have been forgiven and whose sins have been covered,

text.NRSV: blessed is the one against whom the Lord will not reckon sin.”
text.Rotherham: Happy, the man whose sin the Lord will in nowise reckon.

text.NRSV: Is this blessedness, then, only for the circumcised, or also for the uncircumcised? We say, “Faith was reckoned to Abraham as righteousness.”
text.Rotherham: This happiness, then, [is it] for the circumcision, or for the uncircumcision? for we say—His faith was reckoned unto Abraham as righteousness:

text.NRSV: How then was it reckoned to him? Was it before or after he had been circumcised? It was not after, but before he was circumcised.
text.Rotherham: How, then, was it reckoned? When he was in circumcision or in uncircumcision? Not in circumcision, but in uncircumcision;

text.NRSV: He received the sign of circumcision as a seal of the righteousness that he had by faith while he was still uncircumcised. The purpose was to make him the ancestor of all who believe without being circumcised and who thus have righteousness reckoned to them,
text.Rotherham: And, a sign, he received [namely] of circumcision, a seal of the righteousness of the faith which he had while yet uncircumcised; to the end he might be father of all that believe during uncircumcision, to the end [the same] righteousness might be reckoned unto them,—

text.NRSV: and likewise the ancestor of the circumcised who are not only circumcised but who also follow the example of the faith that our ancestor Abraham had before he was circumcised.
text.Rotherham: And father of circumcision—unto them who are not of circumcision only, but who also walk in the steps of the faith, while yet uncircumcised, of our father Abraham.

text.NRSV: For the promise that he would inherit the world did not come to Abraham or to his descendants through the law but through the righteousness of faith.
text.Rotherham: For, not through means of law, doth the promise belong unto Abraham or unto his seed,—that he should be heir of the world; but, through a righteousness by faith.

text.NRSV: If it is the adherents of the law who are to be the heirs, faith is null and the promise is void.
text.Rotherham: For, if they who are of law are heirs, made void is faith and of no effect is the promise.

text.NRSV: For the law brings wrath; but where there is no law, neither is there violation.
text.Rotherham: For, the law, worketh out anger, but, where there is no law, neither is there transgression.

text.NRSV: For this reason it depends on faith, in order that the promise may rest on grace and be guaranteed to all his descendants, not only to the adherents of the law but also to those who share the faith of Abraham (for he is the father of all of us,
text.Rotherham: For this cause, it is by faith, in order that it may be by way of favour, so that the promise is firm unto all the seed,—not unto that by the law only, but unto that also [which is such] by the faith of Abraham; who is father of us all,—

text.NRSV: as it is written, “I have made you the father of many nations”)—in the presence of the God in whom he believed, who gives life to the dead and calls into existence the things that do not exist.
text.Rotherham: Even as it is written—Father of many nations, have I appointed thee: before him whom he believed—God, who causeth the dead to live, and calleth the things that are not as things that are:—

text.NRSV: Hoping against hope, he believed that he would become “the father of many nations,” according to what was said, “So numerous shall your descendants be.”
text.Rotherham: Who, past hope, upon hope believed, so that he became father of many nations,—according to what had been said—So shall be thy seed;—

text.NRSV: He did not weaken in faith when he considered his own body, which was already as good as dead (for he was about a hundred years old), or when he considered the barrenness of Sarah’s womb.
text.Rotherham: And, without becoming weak in his faith, he attentively considered his own body, already deadened—he being a hundred years old, the deadening also of Sarah’s womb;

text.NRSV: No distrust made him waver concerning the promise of God, but he grew strong in his faith as he gave glory to God,
text.Rotherham: In respect, however, of the promise of God, he was not led to hesitate by unbelief, but received power by his faith, giving glory unto God,

text.NRSV: being fully convinced that God was able to do what he had promised.
text.Rotherham: And being fully persuaded (that),—what he hath promised, able is he also to perform:

text.NRSV: Therefore it was “reckoned to him as righteousness.”
text.Rotherham: Wherefore [also], it was reckoned unto him as righteousness.

text.NRSV: Now the words, “it was reckoned to him,” were written not for his sake alone,
text.Rotherham: Now it was not written for his sake alone that it was reckoned unto him,

text.NRSV: but for ours also. It will be reckoned to us who believe in him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead,
text.Rotherham: But for our sakes also—unto whom it is to be reckoned,—even unto them that believe upon him who raised Jesus our Lord from among the dead:

text.NRSV: who was handed over to death for our trespasses and was raised for our justification.
text.Rotherham: Who was delivered up on account of our offences and was raised on account of the declaring us righteous.

text.NRSV: Therefore, since we are justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ,
text.Rotherham: Having, therefore, been declared righteous by faith, let us have, peace, towards God, through our Lord Jesus Christ,—

text.NRSV: through whom we have obtained access to this grace in which we stand; and we boast in our hope of sharing the glory of God.
text.Rotherham: Through whom also we have had, our introduction, [by our faith] into this favour wherein we stand; and let us boast in hope of the glory of God.

text.NRSV: And not only that, but we also boast in our sufferings, knowing that suffering produces endurance,
text.Rotherham: And, not only so, but let us boast also in our tribulations; knowing that, our tribulation, worketh out endurance.

text.NRSV: and endurance produces character, and character produces hope,
text.Rotherham: And, our endurance, a testing, and, our testing, hope,

text.NRSV: and hope does not disappoint us, because God’s love has been poured into our hearts through the Holy Spirit that has been given to us.
text.Rotherham: And, our hope, putteth not to shame. Because, the love of God, hath been poured out in our hearts, through the Holy Spirit that hath been given unto us:

text.NRSV: For while we were still weak, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly.
text.Rotherham: Seeing that, Christ, we being weak as yet, seasonably, in behalf of such as were ungodly, died.

text.NRSV: Indeed, rarely will anyone die for a righteous person—though perhaps for a good person someone might actually dare to die.
text.Rotherham: For, scarcely in behalf of a righteous man, will one die,—in behalf of the good man indeed, peradventure one even dareth to die;

text.NRSV: But God proves his love for us in that while we still were sinners Christ died for us.
text.Rotherham: But God commendeth his own love unto us in that—we as yet being sinners, Christ in our behalf died.

text.NRSV: Much more surely then, now that we have been justified by his blood, will we be saved through him from the wrath of God.
text.Rotherham: Much more, then, having now been declared righteous by his blood, shall we be saved through him from the anger.

text.NRSV: For if while we were enemies, we were reconciled to God through the death of his Son, much more surely, having been reconciled, will we be saved by his life.
text.Rotherham: For, if being enemies we were reconciled unto God through the death of his Son, much more, having been reconciled, shall we be saved by his life.

text.NRSV: But more than that, we even boast in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have now received reconciliation.
text.Rotherham: And, not only, so, but are even boasting in God, through our Lord Jesus [Christ],—through whom, now, the reconciliation we have received.

text.NRSV: Therefore, just as sin came into the world through one man, and death came through sin, and so death spread to all because all have sinned—
text.Rotherham: For this cause,—just as, through one man, sin into the world entered, and through sin, death,—and, so, unto all men death passed through, for that all had sinned;—

text.NRSV: sin was indeed in the world before the law, but sin is not reckoned when there is no law.
text.Rotherham: For, until law, sin was in the world, although sin is not reckoned when there is no law,—

text.NRSV: Yet death exercised dominion from Adam to Moses, even over those whose sins were not like the transgression of Adam, who is a type of the one who was to come.
text.Rotherham: Yet still, death reigned from Adam until Moses, even over them who had not sinned after the likeness of the transgression of Adam,—who is a type of the Coming One;—

text.NRSV: But the free gift is not like the trespass. For if the many died through the one man’s trespass, much more surely have the grace of God and the free gift in the grace of the one man, Jesus Christ, abounded for the many.
text.Rotherham: But, not as the fault, so, [also] the decree of favour, for, if, by the fault of the one, the many died, much more, the favour of God and the free-gift in favour, by the one man Jesus Christ, unto the many superabounded;

text.NRSV: And the free gift is not like the effect of the one man’s sin. For the judgment following one trespass brought condemnation, but the free gift following many trespasses brings justification.
text.Rotherham: And, not as through one that sinned, is that which is freely given, for, the sentence of judgment, indeed, was—out of one [fault] into condemnation, whereas, the decree of favour, is—out of many faults, into a recovery of righteousness.

text.NRSV: If, because of the one man’s trespass, death exercised dominion through that one, much more surely will those who receive the abundance of grace and the free gift of righteousness exercise dominion in life through the one man, Jesus Christ.
text.Rotherham: For, if, by the fault of the one, death reigned through the one, much more, they who the superabundance of the favour and of [the free-gift of] the righteousness do receive, in life, shall reign through the one, Jesus Christ.

text.NRSV: Therefore just as one man’s trespass led to condemnation for all, so one man’s act of righteousness leads to justification and life for all.
text.Rotherham: Hence then, as through one fault, [the sentence was] unto all men unto condemnation, so, also, through one recovery of righteousness, [the decree of favour] is unto all men for righteous acquittal unto life;

text.NRSV: For just as by the one man’s disobedience the many were made sinners, so by the one man’s obedience the many will be made righteous.
text.Rotherham: For, just as, through the disobedience of the one man, sinners, the many were constituted, so, also, through the obedience of the one, righteous, the many shall be constituted—

text.NRSV: But law came in, with the result that the trespass multiplied; but where sin increased, grace abounded all the more,
text.Rotherham: Law, however, gained admission, in order that the fault might abound, but, where the sin abounded, the favour greatly superabounded:

text.NRSV: so that, just as sin exercised dominion in death, so grace might also exercise dominion through justification leading to eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
text.Rotherham: In order that—just as sin reigned in death, so, also, favour, might reign through righteousness unto life age-abiding, through Jesus Christ our Lord.

text.NRSV: What then are we to say? Should we continue in sin in order that grace may abound?
text.Rotherham: What then shall we say? are we still to continue in sin that favour may abound?

text.NRSV: By no means! How can we who died to sin go on living in it?
text.Rotherham: Far be it! we who have died unto sin, how, any longer, shall we live therein?

text.NRSV: Do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death?
text.Rotherham: Or know ye not that, we, as many as were immersed into Christ [Jesus], into his death were immersed?

text.NRSV: Therefore we have been buried with him by baptism into death, so that, just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glory of the Father, so we too might walk in newness of life.
text.Rotherham: We were, therefore, buried together with him through our immersion into his death, in order that—just as Christ was raised from among the dead through the glory of the Father, so, we also, in newness of life should walk.

text.NRSV: For if we have been united with him in a death like his, we will certainly be united with him in a resurrection like his.
text.Rotherham: For, if we have come to be grown together in the likeness of his death, certainly, in that of his resurrection also, shall we be.

text.NRSV: We know that our old self was crucified with him so that the body of sin might be destroyed, and we might no longer be enslaved to sin.
text.Rotherham: Of this taking note—that, our old man, was crucified together with him in order that the sinful body might be made powerless, that we should, no longer, be in servitude to sin;

text.NRSV: For whoever has died is freed from sin.
text.Rotherham: For, he that hath died, hath become righteously acquitted from his sin.

text.NRSV: But if we have died with Christ, we believe that we will also live with him.
text.Rotherham: Now, if we have died together with Christ, we believe that we shall also live together with him;

text.NRSV: We know that Christ, being raised from the dead, will never die again; death no longer has dominion over him.
text.Rotherham: Knowing that, Christ having been raised from among the dead, no more dieth,—death, over him, no more, hath lordship,—

text.NRSV: The death he died, he died to sin, once for all; but the life he lives, he lives to God.
text.Rotherham: For, in that he died, unto sin, died he once for all, but, in that he liveth, he liveth unto God.

text.NRSV: So you also must consider yourselves dead to sin and alive to God in Christ Jesus.
text.Rotherham: So, ye also, be reckoning yourselves to be—dead indeed unto sin, but, alive unto God in Christ Jesus.

text.NRSV: Therefore, do not let sin exercise dominion in your mortal bodies, to make you obey their passions.
text.Rotherham: Let not sin, therefore, reign in your death-doomed body, that ye should be obedient to its covetings;

text.NRSV: No longer present your members to sin as instruments of wickedness, but present yourselves to God as those who have been brought from death to life, and your members to God as instruments of righteousness.
text.Rotherham: Neither be presenting your members as weapons of unrighteousness unto sin, but present yourselves unto God as though alive from among the dead, and your members as weapons of righteousness unto God;

text.NRSV: For sin will have no dominion over you, since you are not under law but under grace.
text.Rotherham: For, sin, over you, shall not have lordship, for ye are not under law, but under favour.

text.NRSV: What then? Should we sin because we are not under law but under grace? By no means!
text.Rotherham: What then? Shall we sin, because we are not under law but under favour? Far be it!

text.NRSV: Do you not know that if you present yourselves to anyone as obedient slaves, you are slaves of the one whom you obey, either of sin, which leads to death, or of obedience, which leads to righteousness?
text.Rotherham: Know ye not that, unto whom ye are presenting yourselves as servants for obedience, servants ye are unto [him unto] whom ye are obedient, whether of sin unto death or of obedience unto righteousness?

text.NRSV: But thanks be to God that you, having once been slaves of sin, have become obedient from the heart to the form of teaching to which you were entrusted,
text.Rotherham: But thanks be unto God, that—whereas ye were servants of sin, ye became obedient out of the heart unto the mould of teaching into which ye were delivered;

text.NRSV: and that you, having been set free from sin, have become slaves of righteousness.
text.Rotherham: And, being freed from sin, ye were made servants unto righteousness;—

text.NRSV: I am speaking in human terms because of your natural limitations. For just as you once presented your members as slaves to impurity and to greater and greater iniquity, so now present your members as slaves to righteousness for sanctification.
text.Rotherham: In human fashion am I speaking, because of the weakness of your flesh;—for, just as ye presented your members as servants unto impurity and unto lawlessness [for lawlessness], so now, present ye your members as servants unto righteousness for sanctification.

text.NRSV: When you were slaves of sin, you were free in regard to righteousness.
text.Rotherham: For, when ye were servants of sin, ye were free as to righteousness;—

text.NRSV: So what advantage did you then get from the things of which you are now ashamed? The end of those things is death.
text.Rotherham: What fruit, therefore, had ye then—in things for which ye now are taking shame to yourselves? For, the end of those things, is death.

text.NRSV: But now that you have been set free from sin and have become slaves of God, the advantage you get is sanctification. The end is eternal life.
text.Rotherham: Whereas, now, having been freed from sin, and made servants unto God, ye have your fruit for sanctification and, the end, life age-abiding.

text.NRSV: For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.
text.Rotherham: For, the wages of sin, is death; but, God’s gift of favour, is life age-abiding, in Christ Jesus our Lord.

text.NRSV: Do you not know, brothers and sisters—for I am speaking to those who know the law—that the law is binding on a person only that person is alive?
text.Rotherham: Or are ye ignorant, brethren,—for unto them that understand the law am I speaking, that, the law, hath lordship over a men as long as he liveth?

text.NRSV: Thus a married woman is bound by the law to her husband as long as he lives; but if her husband dies, she is discharged from the law concerning the husband.
text.Rotherham: For, the married woman, unto her living husband is bound by law; but, if her husband have died, she hath received a full release from the law of her husband.

text.NRSV: Accordingly, she will be called an adulteress if she lives with another man while her husband is alive. But if her husband dies, she is free from that law, and if she marries another man, she is not an adulteress.
text.Rotherham: Hence then, her husband being alive, an adulteress, shall she be called—if she become another man’s, but, if the husband have died, she is free from the law; so that she is not an adulteress, though she become another man’s.

text.NRSV: In the same way, my friends, you have died to the law through the body of Christ, so that you may belong to another, to him who has been raised from the dead in order that we may bear fruit for God.
text.Rotherham: So, then, my brethren, ye also, were made dead unto the law through the body of the Christ, to the end ye might become another’s—his who from among the dead was raised, in order that we might bring forth fruit unto God.

text.NRSV: While we were living in the flesh, our sinful passions, aroused by the law, were at work in our members to bear fruit for death.
text.Rotherham: For, when we were in the flesh, the susceptibilities of sins which were through the law, used to be energized in our members unto the bringing forth of fruit unto death;

text.NRSV: But now we are discharged from the law, dead to that which held us captive, so that we are slaves not under the old written code but in the new life of the Spirit.
text.Rotherham: But, now, we have received full release from the law, by dying [in that] wherein we used to be held fast, so that we should be doing service—in newness of spirit and not in obsoleteness of letter.

text.NRSV: What then should we say? That the law is sin? By no means! Yet, if it had not been for the law, I would not have known sin. I would not have known what it is to covet if the law had not said, “You shall not covet.”
text.Rotherham: What, then, shall we say? Is the law sin? Far be it! On the contrary, I had not discovered, sin, save through law, for even, of coveting, I had not been aware if, the law, had not kept on saying—Thou shall not covet;

text.NRSV: But sin, seizing an opportunity in the commandment, produced in me all kinds of covetousness. Apart from the law sin lies dead.
text.Rotherham: Howbeit sin taking, occasion—through the commandment, wrought out in me all manner of coveting; for, apart from law, sin is dead;—

text.NRSV: I was once alive apart from the law, but when the commandment came, sin revived
text.Rotherham: And, I, was alive, apart from law, at one time, but, the commandment coming, sin sprang up to life,

text.NRSV: and I died, and the very commandment that promised life proved to be death to me.
text.Rotherham: Whereas, I, died,—and the commandment which was unto life was found by me to be, itself, unto death;

text.NRSV: For sin, seizing an opportunity in the commandment, deceived me and through it killed me.
text.Rotherham: For, sin,—taking occasion—through the commandment, completely deceived me and, through it, slew me:

text.NRSV: So the law is holy, and the commandment is holy and just and good.
text.Rotherham: So that, the law, indeed, is holy, and the commandment, holy, and righteous and good.

text.NRSV: Did what is good, then, bring death to me? By no means! It was sin, working death in me through what is good, in order that sin might be shown to be sin, and through the commandment might become sinful beyond measure.
text.Rotherham: Did, then, that which is good, unto me become death? Far be it! But [it was] sin, that it might appear sin, through that which was good unto me working out death, in order that, exceeding sinful, might sin become through the commandment.

text.NRSV: For we know that the law is spiritual; but I am of the flesh, sold into slavery under sin.
text.Rotherham: For we know that, the law, is spiritual,—I, however, am a creature of flesh, sold under sin;

text.NRSV: I do not understand my own actions. For I do not do what I want, but I do the very thing I hate.
text.Rotherham: For, that which I am working out, I do not approve,—for not, what I wish, the same I practise, but, what I hate, the same I do:

text.NRSV: Now if I do what I do not want, I agree that the law is good.
text.Rotherham: Now, if what I wish not the same I do, I consent unto the law that [it is] right.

text.NRSV: But in fact it is no longer I that do it, but sin that dwells within me.
text.Rotherham: Now, however, no longer am, I, working it out, but the, sin, that dwelleth in me:

text.NRSV: For I know that nothing good dwells within me, that is, in my flesh. I can will what is right, but I cannot do it.
text.Rotherham: I know, in fact, that there dwelleth not in me, that is, in my flesh, anything good; for, the wishing, lieth near me, but, the working out of what is right, not!

text.NRSV: For I do not do the good I want, but the evil I do not want is what I do.
text.Rotherham: For not, the good that I wish, I do, but, the evil that I do not wish, the same I practise.

text.NRSV: Now if I do what I do not want, it is no longer I that do it, but sin that dwells within me.
text.Rotherham: Now, if what I wish not, the same, I do, no longer am, I, working it out, but the, sin, that dwelleth in me.

text.NRSV: So I find it to be a law that when I want to do what is good, evil lies close at hand.
text.Rotherham: Hence, I find the law, to me who wish to be doing the right, that, unto me, the wrong lieth near:

text.NRSV: For I delight in the law of God in my inmost self,
text.Rotherham: I have, in fact, a sympathetic pleasure in the law of God; according to the inner man.

text.NRSV: but I see in my members another law at war with the law of my mind, making me captive to the law of sin that dwells in my members.
text.Rotherham: But I behold a diverse law in my members, warring against the law of my mind and taking me captive in the law of sin which existeth in my members:—

text.NRSV: Wretched man that I am! Who will rescue me from this body of death?
text.Rotherham: Wretched, man am, I! Who shall rescue me out of this body doomed to death?

text.NRSV: Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord! So then, with my mind I am a slave to the law of God, but with my flesh I am a slave to the law of sin.
text.Rotherham: [But] thanks be unto God!—Through Jesus Christ our Lord. Hence, then,—I myself, with the mind, indeed, am in servitude unto a law of God; but; with the flesh; unto a law of sin.

text.NRSV: There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.
text.Rotherham: Hence there is now, no, condemnation unto them who are in Christ Jesus;

text.NRSV: For the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus has set you free from the law of sin and of death.
text.Rotherham: For, the law of the spirit of life in Christ Jesus, hath set thee free from the law of sin and of death;—

text.NRSV: For God has done what the law, weakened by the flesh, could not do: by sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh, and to deal with sin, he condemned sin in the flesh,
text.Rotherham: For, what was impossible by the law in that it was weak through the flesh, God, by sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh and concerning sin, condemned sin in the flesh,

text.NRSV: so that the just requirement of the law might be fulfilled in us, who walk not according to the flesh but according to the Spirit.
text.Rotherham: In order that, the righteous requirement of the law, might be fulfilled in us—who, not according to flesh, do walk, but according to spirit;

text.NRSV: For those who live according to the flesh set their minds on the things of the flesh, but those who live according to the Spirit set their minds on the things of the Spirit.
text.Rotherham: For, they who according to flesh have their being, the things of the flesh do prefer, but, they according to the spirit, the things of the spirit;

text.NRSV: To set the mind on the flesh is death, but to set the mind on the Spirit is life and peace.
text.Rotherham: For, what is preferred by the flesh, [is] death, whereas, what is preferred by the spirit, [is] life and peace;—

text.NRSV: For this reason the mind that is set on the flesh is hostile to God; it does not submit to God’s law—indeed it cannot,
text.Rotherham: Inasmuch as, what is preferred by the flesh, [is] hostile towards God, for, unto the law of God, it doth not submit itself, neither in fact can it.—

text.NRSV: and those who are in the flesh cannot please God.
text.Rotherham: They, moreover, who in flesh have their being, cannot please, God.

text.NRSV: But you are not in the flesh; you are in the Spirit, since the Spirit of God dwells in you. Anyone who does not have the Spirit of Christ does not belong to him.
text.Rotherham: But, ye, have not your being in flesh, but in spirit,—if at least, God’s Spirit, dwelleth in you; and, if anyone hath not Christ’s Spirit, the same, is not his;—

text.NRSV: But if Christ is in you, though the body is dead because of sin, the Spirit is life because of righteousness.
text.Rotherham: But, if Christ is in you, the body, indeed, is dead by reason of sin, whereas, the spirit, is life by reason of righteousness;

text.NRSV: If the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you, he who raised Christ from the dead will give life to your mortal bodies also through his Spirit that dwells in you.
text.Rotherham: If, moreover, the Spirit of him that raised Jesus from among the dead dwelleth in you, he that raised from among the dead Christ Jesus, shall make alive [even] your death-doomed bodies, through means of his indwelling Spirit within you.

text.NRSV: So then, brothers and sisters, we are debtors, not to the flesh, to live according to the flesh—
text.Rotherham: Hence, then, brethren—debtors, we are, not unto the flesh, that, according to flesh, we should live,—

text.NRSV: for if you live according to the flesh, you will die; but if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live.
text.Rotherham: For, if according to flesh ye live, ye are about to die, whereas, if in spirit, the practices of the flesh, ye are putting to death, ye shall attain unto life;

text.NRSV: For all who are led by the Spirit of God are children of God.
text.Rotherham: For, as many as by God’s Spirit are being led, the same, are God’s sons,—

text.NRSV: For you did not receive a spirit of slavery to fall back into fear, but you have received a spirit of adoption. When we cry, “Abba! Father!”
text.Rotherham: For ye have not received a spirit of servitude, leading back into fear, but ye have received a spirit of sonship, whereby we are exclaiming—Abba! Oh Father!

text.NRSV: it is that very Spirit bearing witness with our spirit that we are children of God,
text.Rotherham: The Spirit itself, beareth witness together with our spirit that we are children of God;

text.NRSV: and if children, then heirs, heirs of God and joint heirs with Christ—if, in fact, we suffer with him so that we may also be glorified with him.
text.Rotherham: And, if children, heirs also—heirs, indeed, of God, but co-heirs with Christ,—if, at least, we are suffering together, in order that we may also be glorified together.

text.NRSV: I consider that the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory about to be revealed to us.
text.Rotherham: For I reckon that unworthy are the sufferings of the present season to be compared with the glory about to be revealed towards us;

text.NRSV: For the creation waits with eager longing for the revealing of the children of God;
text.Rotherham: For, the eager outlook of creation, ardently awaiteth the revealing of the sons of God,—

text.NRSV: for the creation was subjected to futility, not of its own will but by the will of the one who subjected it, in hope
text.Rotherham: For, unto vanity, hath creation been made subject—not by choice, but by reason of him that made it subject, in hope

text.NRSV: that the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to decay and will obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God.
text.Rotherham: That, creation itself also, shall be freed—from the bondage of the decay into the freedom of the glory of the sons of God;

text.NRSV: We know that the whole creation has been groaning in labor pains until now;
text.Rotherham: For we know that, all creation, is sighing together, and travailing-in-birth-throes together until the present,—

text.NRSV: and not only the creation, but we ourselves, who have the first fruits of the Spirit, groan inwardly while we wait for adoption, the redemption of our bodies.
text.Rotherham: And, not only so, but, we ourselves, also, who have the first-fruit of the Spirit—[we] even ourselves, within our own selves do sigh,—sonship ardently awaiting—the redeeming of our body;—

text.NRSV: For in hope we were saved. Now hope that is seen is not hope. For who hopes for what is seen?
text.Rotherham: For, by our hope, have we been saved,—but, hope beheld, is not hope, for, what one beholdeth, why doth he hope for?

text.NRSV: But if we hope for what we do not see, we wait for it with patience.
text.Rotherham: If, however, what we do not behold we hope for, with endurance, are we ardently awaiting [it] ;—

text.NRSV: Likewise the Spirit helps us in our weakness; for we do not know how to pray as we ought, but that very Spirit intercedes with sighs too deep for words.
text.Rotherham: In the selfsame way moreover, even the Spirit, helpeth together in our weakness,—for, what we should pray for as we ought, we know not, but, the Spirit itself, maketh intercession with sighings unutterable,

text.NRSV: And God, who searches the heart, knows what is the mind of the Spirit, because the Spirit intercedes for the saints according to the will of God.
text.Rotherham: And, he that searcheth the hearts, knoweth what is preferred by the Spirit—that, according to God, he maketh intercession in behalf of saints;

text.NRSV: We know that all things work together for good for those who love God, who are called according to his purpose.
text.Rotherham: We know, further, that, unto them who love God, God causeth all things to work together for good,—unto them who, according to purpose, are such as he hath called;

text.NRSV: For those whom he foreknew he also predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son, in order that he might be the firstborn within a large family.
text.Rotherham: For, whom he fore-approved, he also fore-appointed to be conformed unto the image of his Son, that he might be firstborn among many brethren,—

text.NRSV: And those whom he predestined he also called; and those whom he called he also justified; and those whom he justified he also glorified.
text.Rotherham: And, whom he fore-appointed, the same, he also called, and, whom he called, the same, he also declared righteous, and, whom he declared righteous, the same, he also made glorious:—

text.NRSV: What then are we to say about these things? If God is for us, who is against us?
text.Rotherham: What, then, shall we say to a these things? If God [is] for us, who [shall be] against us?

text.NRSV: He who did not withhold his own Son, but gave him up for all of us, will he not with him also give us everything else?
text.Rotherham: He, at least, who his own Son did not spare, but in behalf of us all delivered him up, how shall he not also, with him, all things upon us in favour bestow?

text.NRSV: Who will bring any charge against God’s elect? It is God who justifies.
text.Rotherham: Who shall bring an accusation against the chosen ones of God? God, who declareth righteous?

text.NRSV: Who is to condemn? It is Christ Jesus, who died, yes, who was raised, who is at the right hand of God, who indeed intercedes for us.
text.Rotherham: Who is he that condemneth? Christ [Jesus] who died?—Nay! rather was raised [from among the dead],—who is on the right hand of God, who also is making intercession in our behalf?

text.NRSV: Who will separate us from the love of Christ? Will hardship, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or peril, or sword?
text.Rotherham: Who shall separate us from the love of the Christ? Shall tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or peril, or sword?—

text.NRSV: As it is written, “For your sake we are being killed all day long; we are accounted as sheep to be slaughtered.”
text.Rotherham: According as it is written—For thy sake, are we being put to death all the day long, we have been reckoned as sheep for slaughter.—

text.NRSV: No, in all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us.
text.Rotherham: Nay, in all these things, we are more than conquering through him that hath loved us.

text.NRSV: For I am convinced that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor rulers, nor things present, nor things to come, nor powers,
text.Rotherham: For I am persuaded that—neither death nor life, nor messengers nor principalities, nor things present nor things to come, nor powers,

text.NRSV: nor height, nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord.
text.Rotherham: Nor height nor depth, nor any other created thing, shall be able to separate us from the love of God which is in Christ Jesus our Lord.

text.NRSV: I am speaking the truth in Christ—I am not lying; my conscience confirms it by the Holy Spirit—
text.Rotherham: Truth, say I, in Christ, I utter no falsehood,—my conscience bearing witness with me in the Holy Spirit,—

text.NRSV: I have great sorrow and unceasing anguish in my heart.
text.Rotherham: That I have great grief and incessant travail in my heart;

text.NRSV: For I could wish that I myself were accursed and cut off from Christ for the sake of my own people, my kindred according to the flesh.
text.Rotherham: For I could have wished to be accursed, even I myself, from the Christ, in behalf of my brethren my kinsmen according to the flesh;—

text.NRSV: They are Israelites, and to them belong the adoption, the glory, the covenants, the giving of the law, the worship, and the promises;
text.Rotherham: Who, indeed, are Israelites, whose are the sonship, and the glory, and the covenants, and the legislation, and the divine service, and the promises,

text.NRSV: to them belong the patriarchs, and from them, according to the flesh, comes the Messiah, who is over all, God blessed forever. Amen.
text.Rotherham: Whose are the fathers, and of whom is the Christ—according to the flesh—he who is over all, God, blessed unto the ages. Amen.

text.NRSV: It is not as though the word of God had failed. For not all Israelites truly belong to Israel,
text.Rotherham: It is not, however, as though the word of God had failed; for, not all they who are of Israel, the same are Israel.

text.NRSV: and not all of Abraham’s children are his true descendants; but “It is through Isaac that descendants shall be named for you.”
text.Rotherham: Neither, because they are seed of Abraham, are all children,—but, In Isaac, shall there be called unto thee a seed.

text.NRSV: This means that it is not the children of the flesh who are the children of God, but the children of the promise are counted as descendants.
text.Rotherham: That is—not the children of the flesh, the same are children of God; but, the children of the promise, are reckoned as a seed.

text.NRSV: For this is what the promise said, “About this time I will return and Sarah shall have a son.”
text.Rotherham: For, of promise, is this word—According to this season, will I come, and Sarah shall have a son.

text.NRSV: Nor is that all; something similar happened to Rebecca when she had conceived children by one husband, our ancestor Isaac.
text.Rotherham: And, not only so, but, when, Rebekah also, was with child, of one—Isaac our father,—

text.NRSV: Even before they had been born or had done anything good or bad (so that God’s purpose of election might continue,
text.Rotherham: They, in fact, not being yet born, nor having practised anything good or bad,—in order that the purpose of God by way of election might stand,—not by works but by him that was calling,

text.NRSV: not by works but by his call) she was told, “The elder shall serve the younger.”
text.Rotherham: It was said unto her—The elder, shall serve the younger;

text.NRSV: As it is written, “I have loved Jacob, but I have hated Esau.”
text.Rotherham: Even as it is written—Jacob, have I loved, but, Esau, have I hated.

text.NRSV: What then are we to say? Is there injustice on God’s part? By no means!
text.Rotherham: What, then, shall we say? Is there injustice with God? Far be it!

text.NRSV: For he says to Moses, “I will have mercy on whom I have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion.”
text.Rotherham: For, unto Moses, he saith—I will have mercy upon whomsoever I can have mercy, and I will have compassion upon whomsoever I can have compassion.

text.NRSV: So it depends not on human will or exertion, but on God who shows mercy.
text.Rotherham: Hence, then, it is nor of him that wisheth nor of him that runneth, but of the mercy-shewing God.

text.NRSV: For the scripture says to Pharaoh, “I have raised you up for the very purpose of showing my power in you, so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth.”
text.Rotherham: For the Scripture saith unto Pharaoh—Unto this end, have I raised thee up, that I may thus shew in thee my power, and that I may declare my name in all the earth.

text.NRSV: So then he has mercy on whomever he chooses, and he hardens the heart of whomever he chooses.
text.Rotherham: Hence, then,—on whom he pleaseth, he hath mercy, and, whom he pleaseth, he doth harden.

text.NRSV: You will say to me then, “Why then does he still find fault? For who can resist his will?”
text.Rotherham: Thou wilt say to me, then—Why longer findeth he fault? For, his purpose, who hath withstood?

text.NRSV: But who indeed are you, a human being, to argue with God? Will what is molded say to the one who molded it, “Why have you made me like this?”
text.Rotherham: O man! Who, nevertheless, art, thou, that art answering again unto God? Shall the thing formed say unto him that formed it—Why didst thou make me thus?

text.NRSV: Has the potter no right over the clay, to make out of the same lump one object for special use and another for ordinary use?
text.Rotherham: Or hath not the potter a right over the clay—out of the same lump, to make some, indeed, into a vessel for honour, and some for dishonour?

text.NRSV: What if God, desiring to show his wrath and to make known his power, has endured with much patience the objects of wrath that are made for destruction;
text.Rotherham: And, if God—wishing to shew his anger and to make known his power—bare, in much patience, with vessels of anger already fitted for destruction,

text.NRSV: and what if he has done so in order to make known the riches of his glory for the objects of mercy, which he has prepared beforehand for glory—
text.Rotherham: In order that he might make known the riches of his glory upon vessels of mercy which he prepared beforehand for glory,—

text.NRSV: including us whom he has called, not from the Jews only but also from the Gentiles?
text.Rotherham: Whom he also called, even us, not only from among Jews, but also from among the nations, [what then?]

text.NRSV: As indeed he says in Hosea, “Those who were not my people I will call ‘my people,’ and her who was not beloved I will call ‘beloved.’”
text.Rotherham: As also in Hosea he saith—I will call the not-my-people, My people, and the not-beloved, beloved,

text.NRSV: “And in the very place where it was said to them, ‘You are not my people,’ there they shall be called children of the living God.”
text.Rotherham: And it shall be—in the place where it was said [to them]—not my people are, ye! there, shall they be called—Sons of a Living God.

text.NRSV: And Isaiah cries out concerning Israel, “Though the number of the children of Israel were like the sand of the sea, only a remnant of them will be saved;
text.Rotherham: Isaiah, moreover, exclaimeth over Israel—Though the number of the sons of Israel be as the sand of the sea, the remnant, shall be saved;

text.NRSV: for the Lord will execute his sentence on the earth quickly and decisively.”
text.Rotherham: For, a complete and concise account, will the Lord make upon the earth.

text.NRSV: And as Isaiah predicted, “If the Lord of hosts had not left survivors to us, we would have fared like Sodom and been made like Gomorrah.”
text.Rotherham: And, even as Isaiah hath before said,—If, the Lord of hosts, had not left us a seed, as Sodom, had we become, and, as Gomorrha, had we been made like.

text.NRSV: What then are we to say? Gentiles, who did not strive for righteousness, have attained it, that is, righteousness through faith;
text.Rotherham: What, then, shall we say? That, they of the nations, who were not in pursuit of righteousness, have laid hold of righteousness,—a righteousness, however, which is by faith;

text.NRSV: but Israel, who did strive for the righteousness that is based on the law, did not succeed in fulfilling that law.
text.Rotherham: Whereas, Israel, though in pursuit of a law of righteousness, unto a law, have not attained.

text.NRSV: Why not? Because they did not strive for it on the basis of faith, but as if it were based on works. They have stumbled over the stumbling stone,
text.Rotherham: Wherefore? Because, not by faith but as by works, [have they sought it] : they have stumbled at the stone of stumbling,

text.NRSV: as it is written, “See, I am laying in Zion a stone that will make people stumble, a rock that will make them fall, and whoever believes in him will not be put to shame.”
text.Rotherham: Even as it is written—Lo! I lay in Zion, a stone to strike against and a rock to stumble over, and, he that resteth faith thereupon, shall not be put to shame.

text.NRSV: Brothers and sisters, my heart’s desire and prayer to God for them is that they may be saved.
text.Rotherham: Brethren! the delight of my own heart and my supplication God-ward in their behalf, are for salvation;

text.NRSV: I can testify that they have a zeal for God, but it is not enlightened.
text.Rotherham: For I bear them witness that they have, a zeal for God,—but not according to correct knowledge;

text.NRSV: For, being ignorant of the righteousness that comes from God, and seeking to establish their own, they have not submitted to God’s righteousness.
text.Rotherham: For, not knowing God’s righteousness and, their own, seeking to establish, unto the righteousness of God, have they not submitted;

text.NRSV: For Christ is the end of the law so that there may be righteousness for everyone who believes.
text.Rotherham: For Christ is an end of law, for righteousness, unto every one that believeth.

text.NRSV: Moses writes concerning the righteousness that comes from the law, that “the person who does these things will live by them.”
text.Rotherham: For, Moses, writeth that—as touching the righteousness that is by law, The man that hath done [it], shall live thereby;

text.NRSV: But the righteousness that comes from faith says, “Do not say in your heart, ‘Who will ascend into heaven?’ ” (that is, to bring Christ down)
text.Rotherham: Whereas, the righteousness by faith, thus speaketh—Do not say in thy heart, Who shall ascend into heaven? That is, to bring, Christ, down,

text.NRSV: “or ‘Who will descend into the abyss?’ ” (that is, to bring Christ up from the dead).
text.Rotherham: Or, Who shall descend into the abyss? That is, to bring up, Christ, from among the dead;

text.NRSV: But what does it say? “The word is near you, on your lips and in your heart” (that is, the word of faith that we proclaim);
text.Rotherham: But what saith it? Near thee, is the declaration, In thy mouth and in thy heart,—that is, the declaration of the faith, which we proclaim:—

text.NRSV: because if you confess with your lips that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved.
text.Rotherham: That, if thou shalt confess the declaration with thy mouth—That Jesus is, Lord, and shalt believe with thy hear—That, God, raised him from among the dead, thou shalt be saved;—

text.NRSV: For one believes with the heart and so is justified, and one confesses with the mouth and so is saved.
text.Rotherham: For, in heart, it is to be believed unto righteousness, and, by mouth, it is to be confessed unto salvation.

text.NRSV: The scripture says, “No one who believes in him will be put to shame.”
text.Rotherham: For the scripture saith—No one that resteth faith thereupon, shall be put to shame?

text.NRSV: For there is no distinction between Jew and Greek; the same Lord is Lord of all and is generous to all who call on him.
text.Rotherham: For there is no distinction—of Jew or Greek,—for, the same [Lord], is Lord of all, being rich unto all who call upon him;

text.NRSV: For, “Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord shall be saved.”
text.Rotherham: For, Whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord, shall be saved:—

text.NRSV: But how are they to call on one in whom they have not believed? And how are they to believe in one of whom they have never heard? And how are they to hear without someone to proclaim him?
text.Rotherham: How, then, shall men call upon one in whom they have not believed? And how shall they believe in one of whom they have not heard? And how shall they hear, without one to proclaim?

text.NRSV: And how are they to proclaim him unless they are sent? As it is written, “How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news!”
text.Rotherham: And how shall they proclaim, except they be sent? Even as it is written—How beautiful the feet of them that bring glad tidings of good things!

text.NRSV: But not all have obeyed the good news; for Isaiah says, “Lord, who has believed our message?”
text.Rotherham: But, not all, have become obedient unto the glad tidings;—for, Isaiah, saith—Lord! who believed what we have heard?

text.NRSV: So faith comes from what is heard, and what is heard comes through the word of Christ.
text.Rotherham: Hence, our faith, cometh by something heard, and, that which is heard, through a declaration of Christ.

text.NRSV: But I ask, have they not heard? Indeed they have; for “Their voice has gone out to all the earth, and their words to the ends of the world.”
text.Rotherham: But I say: Have they not heard? Yea indeed,—into all the earth, hath gone forth their sound, and, unto the ends of the inhabited world, their declarations.

text.NRSV: Again I ask, did Israel not understand? First Moses says, “I will make you jealous of those who are not a nation; with a foolish nation I will make you angry.”
text.Rotherham: But I say: Hath, Israel, not come to know? First, Moses, saith—I, will provoke you to jealousy on account of a no-nation, on account of an undiscerning nation, will I make you very angry.

text.NRSV: Then Isaiah is so bold as to say, “I have been found by those who did not seek me; I have shown myself to those who did not ask for me.”
text.Rotherham: Isaiah, however, waxeth daring, and saith,—I have been found by them who after me were not seeking, manifest have I become unto them who for me were not enquiring;

text.NRSV: But of Israel he says, “All day long I have held out my hands to a disobedient and contrary people.”
text.Rotherham: Whereas, regarding Israel, he saith—All day long, have I stretched forth my hands unto a people unyielding and contradicting.

text.NRSV: I ask, then, has God rejected his people? By no means! I myself am an Israelite, a descendant of Abraham, a member of the tribe of Benjamin.
text.Rotherham: I say, then—Hath God cast off his people? Far be it! For, I also, am an Israelite,—of the seed of Abraham, of the tribe of Benjamin:

text.NRSV: God has not rejected his people whom he foreknew. Do you not know what the scripture says of Elijah, how he pleads with God against Israel?
text.Rotherham: God hath not cast off his people, whom he fore approved. Or know ye not, in [the account of] Elijah what the scripture saith, when he intercedeth with God against Israel?

text.NRSV: “Lord, they have killed your prophets, they have demolished your altars; I alone am left, and they are seeking my life.”
text.Rotherham: Lord! Thy prophets, have they slain, thine altars, have they overthrown, and, I, am left alone, and they are seeking my life!

text.NRSV: But what is the divine reply to him? “I have kept for myself seven thousand who have not bowed the knee to Baal.”
text.Rotherham: But what saith unto him the response? I have left for myself seven thousand men, who, indeed, have not bowed a knee unto Baal.

text.NRSV: So too at the present time there is a remnant, chosen by grace.
text.Rotherham: Thus, then, in the present season also, a remnant, by way of an election of favour, hath come into being.

text.NRSV: But if it is by grace, it is no longer on the basis of works, otherwise grace would no longer be grace.
text.Rotherham: If, however, by favour, no longer of works; else, favour, no longer proveth to be favour!

text.NRSV: What then? Israel failed to obtain what it was seeking. The elect obtained it, but the rest were hardened,
text.Rotherham: What then? That which Israel seeketh after, the same, it hath not obtained:—the election, however, have obtained it, and, the rest, have been hardened;—

text.NRSV: as it is written, “God gave them a sluggish spirit, eyes that would not see and ears that would not hear, down to this very day.”
text.Rotherham: Even as it is written—God hath given unto them a spirit of stupor,—eyes not to see, and ears not to hear,—until this very day;

text.NRSV: And David says, “Let their table become a snare and a trap, a stumbling block and a retribution for them;
text.Rotherham: And, David, saith—Let their table be turned into a snare, and into gin, and into a trap, and into a recompense unto them,

text.NRSV: let their eyes be darkened so that they cannot see, and keep their backs forever bent.”
text.Rotherham: Darkened be their eyes, not to see, and, their back, do thou continually bow down.

text.NRSV: So I ask, have they stumbled so as to fall? By no means! But through their stumbling salvation has come to the Gentiles, so as to make Israel jealous.
text.Rotherham: I say then—Did they stumble in order that they might fall? Far be it! But, by their fall, salvation [hath come] unto the nations, to the end of provoking them to jealousy.

text.NRSV: Now if their stumbling means riches for the world, and if their defeat means riches for Gentiles, how much more will their full inclusion mean!
text.Rotherham: If, moreover, their fall, is the riches of a world, and their loss, the riches of nations, how much rather their fullness?

text.NRSV: Now I am speaking to you Gentiles. Inasmuch then as I am an apostle to the Gentiles, I glorify my ministry
text.Rotherham: Unto you, however, am I speaking,—you of the nations; inasmuch, indeed, then, as, I, am an apostle to the nations, my ministry, I glorify,

text.NRSV: in order to make my own people jealous, and thus save some of them.
text.Rotherham: If by any means I may provoke to jealousy my own flesh, and save some from among them;—

text.NRSV: For if their rejection is the reconciliation of the world, what will their acceptance be but life from the dead?
text.Rotherham: For, if, the casting away of them, hath become the reconciling of a world, what shall, the taking of them in addition, be, but life from among the dead?

text.NRSV: If the part of the dough offered as first fruits is holy, then the whole batch is holy; and if the root is holy, then the branches also are holy.
